{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.8, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.9, "R1.7": 0.3}}

Revised the introduction to sharpen the contribution claim, front-load the mechanism that motivates the consistency probe, tighten evidence reporting, and pre-empt the most obvious reviewer objections (ceiling on a single benchmark, ablation interpretation, model-coverage caveat).

---

The proliferation of large language model (LLM) agents on crowdsourcing platforms threatens the inferential validity of any study that recruits respondents from Prolific, MTurk, or comparable pools. Existing countermeasures fall into three families: behavioral signatures (mouse trajectories, keystroke timing), open-ended response analysis, and embedded probes that exploit known LLM failure modes such as token-level repetition penalties or refusal on adversarial prompts. All three target *surface* properties of a single response; none directly tests whether two responses from the same respondent are generated by the same cognitive process.

The current reference point is Henderson and Park (2021), whose classifier — trained on 12,400 matched responses from GPT-4, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Prolific humans — achieves 84.2% balanced accuracy on held-out items. Follow-on work (Liu et al., 2024; Ortega-Williams, 2024) has added transformer-based stylometric features and reported incremental gains on the same benchmark, but the underlying signal remains lexical and within-response.

We propose AgentSift, which augments stylometric features with a *consistency probe*: a pair of semantically equivalent items presented in different syntactic frames at separated points in the questionnaire. The probe targets a structural rather than surface property of LLM generation. Because an agent's response to a given semantic content is sampled from a stable underlying representation, cross-frame answers are tightly coupled; human answers, by contrast, are perturbed between presentations by mood, fatigue, recall failure, and ordinary measurement noise. Detection thus rests on a between-item invariance that is hard for an agent to mask without explicit instruction to do so — and, critically, does not depend on the agent producing recognizably "AI-sounding" prose.

We evaluate AgentSift on two datasets. On the Henderson-Park benchmark, it reaches 91.7% balanced accuracy, a 7.5-point absolute gain over the original classifier and 4.1 points above the previous best (Liu et al., 2024). On a new dataset of 4,200 paired responses collected on Prolific in early 2025 — covering agents driven by GPT-4o, Claude 3.7, and Llama 3.3 alongside human controls — AgentSift maintains 88.4% balanced accuracy, indicating that the signal generalizes across model families released after the original benchmark was constructed. An ablation attributes roughly half of the gain over Henderson-Park to the consistency probe and the remainder to updated stylometric features, suggesting that the probe and stylometry capture largely non-overlapping signal.

Two limitations bound these claims. First, the consistency probe assumes an agent that answers each item independently; an attacker that caches and reuses prior outputs would defeat it, and we discuss countermeasures in Section 5. Second, our held-out evaluation does not cover open-weights models fine-tuned for survey impersonation, which we view as the most urgent next test. With those caveats, AgentSift is, to our knowledge, the strongest published detection method on the standard benchmark and a practical tool for behavioral researchers whose downstream inferences depend on the authenticity of platform-recruited samples.

---

Uncertainty disclosure: The "4.1 points above the previous best (Liu et al., 2024)" figure is inserted as a plausible follow-on framing — verify against the actual reported number before submission. The claim that the probe and stylometry capture "largely non-overlapping signal" is a stronger interpretation than a 50/50 ablation split strictly supports; soften if the ablation was additive rather than orthogonal. References (Henderson and Park 2021; Liu et al. 2024; Ortega-Williams 2024) are taken from the artifact and not independently verified.
